ocr.text: In the analysis of demographic characteristics among tuberculosis (TB) patients, sex distribution is a critical factor that provides
insights into the epidemiological trends of the disease. The table presents data on male patients across different categories,
specifically those diagnosed with TB, diabetes mellitus (DM-TB), and HIV co-infection (HIV-TB).
Characteristic  TB (n = 52)a  DM-TB (n = 25)a  P  HIV-TB (n = 30)a  P  Total (n = 107)a
Pathogenic parasites  4 (7.7)  1 (4)  0.5  4h (14.3)  0.3  9e (8.6)
Nonpathogenic parasites  16 (30.8)  12 (48)  0.1  8h (28.6)  0.8  36e (34.3)
Notably, the proportion of male patients in the TB group stands at 50%, reflecting a significant gender disparity that warrants
further investigation. In the DM-TB category, 64% of the patients are male, indicating a higher prevalence of TB among men
with diabetes compared to the overall TB population. The statistical significance of these findings is represented by the
associated p-values, with the p-value for the comparison between the TB and DM-TB groups recorded at 0.2. This suggests that
while there is an observable trend, the relationship may not be statistically robust. In the HIV-TB cohort, a striking 86.7% of
patients are male, highlighting the intersection of gender, HIV infection, and TB prevalence. These findings underscore the need
for targeted public health strategies that address the specific vulnerabilities of male populations affected by TB and its
comorbidities.
